danger_signals.visual_form: Xenomorph sprites (larger character models distinctive to SNES version)
danger_signals.meaning: Primary threat; immediate danger requiring weapon engagement
Secondary threat variant; different attack behavior from adult Aliens
Proximity warning of nearby enemies; world-state shift to heightened danger
Pressure state; failure-condition manifestation of mission urgency
Environmental containment; sealed sections indicate Alien outbreak zones

safety_signals.visual_form: Interactive terminal objects positioned throughout prison colony
safety_signals.meaning: Safe access points; provide mission objectives and blueprint information (tactical safety)

safety_signals.visual_form: NPC entities in secure areas of prison
safety_signals.meaning: Safe zones requiring protection; indicate non-hostile human presence

safety_signals.visual_form: Pulse rifles, flamethrowers, grenades as collectible/equipped items
safety_signals.meaning: Defensive capability; increased player safety through loadout
Safe passage routes through facility; player control over movement

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Gray and metallic-looking prison facility architecture; Fiorina 161 industrial aesthetic; blueprint systems

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Ripley protagonist character model (described as 'skin-headed Ripley' from film)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Prison colony setting; human-scale architecture; NPC prisoners requiring rescue

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Xenomorph and Facehugger visual designs from film license
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Adult Aliens vs. Facehugger life-stage variants; Genesis version features larger Alien character models than SNES
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Outbreak zones; sealed section containment; motion tracker presence indicates territory

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Motion tracker activation; enemy sprites present on screen; sealed doors blocking access
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Ripley must engage combat; time pressure increases; enemy patrol and attack behavior active

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Computer terminals accessible; blueprint displays of prison layout; objective selection interface
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player pauses progression to review tactically; no immediate combat pressure

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: NPC prisoner entities visible in level; doors requiring unlocking; rescue waypoints marked
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Ripley navigates to prisoner locations; secondary objective completion adds to level state

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Multiple sealed doors; restricted passage zones; terminal-accessed restricted areas
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player must solve containment sequences (sealing doors to prevent Alien entry); environmental puzzle solving

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Alien Queen sprite; arena environment change; heightened visual intensity
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Distinct boss-pattern behavior; intensified attack sequences; climactic gameplay state

environmental_storytelling.example: Gray and metallic-looking prison colony architecture communicates industrial confinement setting; Fiorina 161 facility design evokes corporate prison aesthetic
Computer terminals display prison layout blueprints, allowing player to understand facility structure and anticipate threat zones; tactical world-state information
Sealed doors, prisoner locations, and terminal positions communicate mission structure; players learn facility function through objective placement
Scattered arsenals (pulse rifles, flamethrowers, grenades) suggest previous combat or preparation; items in specific locations hint at threat zones
Visible prisoners in certain areas indicate safe zones; absence of human presence in other sections implies Alien-dominated territory

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Trigger motion tracker alert; pursue Ripley when detected; engaged combat pattern
Occupy sealed sections; patrol routes through containment zones; prevent passage through infected areas
Different attack behavior from adult Aliens; secondary enemy variant requiring weapon adaptation
Armed with accessible arsenal; capable of ladder-climbing and door-opening (environmental interaction); responsive to time pressure
Navigates to objective waypoints; accesses computer terminals for briefing; executes containment procedures (door sealing)
Display objective options; provide blueprint information; offer tactical planning interface when approached
Stationary in designated locations; require player escort/completion of rescue objective; indicate safe human areas
Distinct boss-pattern combat sequence; heightened threat manifestation; climactic behavioral state

notes: Alien³ (SNES, 1993) is a run-and-gun adaptation of the film license featuring gray/metallic industrial aesthetic. World-state is primarily communicated through: (1) motion tracker as threat-proximity indicator, (2) sealed doors as outbreak manifestation, (3) computer terminals as safe tactical zones, (4) time limits as urgency pressure. Factions are implicit rather than overt—corporate infrastructure vs. human survivors vs. Alien xenomorphs. Environmental storytelling relies on architecture, objective placement, and NPC positioning. The SNES version differs from Genesis in graphics and design; features fewer levels (6 vs 15) but includes password save feature. No complex political narrative is present; world-state is primarily binary (outbreak active vs. objective-planning phase).
